perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Ownership. The Confidential Information is and shall be considered valuable trade secrets owned exclusively by the Di sclosing Party. The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in the Confidential Information and any reasonably related information which may be subsequently developed, and the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual pr operty and copyright therein in favour of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Ownership. The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in the Confidential Information and any reasonably related information which may be subsequently developed, and the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual pr operty and copyright therein in favour of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause, 'The Confidential Information is and shall be considered valuable trade secrets owned exclusively by the Disclosing Party', this creates ambiguity regarding the legal status of the information. It contradicts section 2.4, which discusses remedies for misappropriation, relying on the 'special, unique and extraordinary character' associated with trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.4 Remedies. The Receiving Party recognises and acknowledges that the Confidential Information is of a special, unique and extraordinary character to the Disclosing Party and the Target and the disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of such Confidential Info rmation by the Receiving Party cannot be fully compensated and that, further, any such disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of the Confidential Information shall cause irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party and/or the Target. The Receiving Party expressly agrees, therefore, that the Disclosing Party, in addition to any rights and remedies it may have under this Agreement or at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent the breach, or the further breach, of any of the provisions of this Ag reement. The Receiving Party agrees to reimburse the Disclosing Party for any and all losses, liabilities, damages, costs, opportunity costs, and direct or indi rect expenses (including reasonable attorney’s fees, profit opportunity returns foregone and any court costs) incurred by the Disclosing Party as a result of any prima facie breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the Confidential Information is owned exclusively by the Disclosing Party and considered a valuable trade secret. The changed text removes this explicit statement of ownership and trade secret status. This creates a contradiction because section 2.4 relies on the Confidential Information being of a 'special, unique and extraordinary character,' which is directly related to the concept of a trade secret. By removing the trade secret designation, the basis for the remedies described in section 2.4 is undermined and contradicted as the 'special, unique, and extraordinary character' is no longer explicitly tied to the information.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2 Disclosure of Confidential Information. The Confidential Information is proprietary information and for the exclusive and non-transferable use of the persons to whom it is addressed a nd the recipients of the Confidential Information agree that all of the informati on contained herein is of a confidential nature and is subject to this Confidenti ality Agreement, and that they will not, directly, or indirectly, disclose or permit their agents or affiliates to disclose any such information without the prior wri tten consent of Transnet except as expressly permitted by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 Disclosure of Confidential Information. The Confidential Information is proprietary information and for the exclusive and non-transferable use of the persons to whom it is addressed a nd the recipients of the Confidential Information agree that they will not, directly, or indirectly, disclose or permit their agents or affiliates to disclose any such information without the prior wri tten consent of Transnet except as expressly permitted by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'all of the information contained herein is of a confidential nature and is subject to this Confidentiality Agreement,' weakens the link between the information and the agreement's restrictions. This change conflicts with section 2.3, which mandates the return of 'all confidential information', because it is not clearly defined what is subject to this return.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.3 Return of Confidential Information. Promptly following the request of the Disclosing Party, the Rece iving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party all confidential information (and copies and ex tracts thereof) furnished to, or created by or on behalf of, the Receiving Part y. Notwithstanding the return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that 'all of the information contained herein is of a confidential nature and is subject to this Confidentiality Agreement'. The changed text removes this crucial phrase. This omission weakens the link between the information and the confidentiality agreement. Consequently, when section 2.3 mandates the return of 'all confidential information', the modified text creates a contradiction because it's no longer clearly defined what information is definitively considered 'confidential' under the agreement, making the return obligation ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition because essential language has been removed, creating a conflict with the obligations in section 2.3.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Return of Confidential Information. Promptly following the request of the Disclosing Party, the Rece iving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party all confidential information (and copies and ex tracts thereof) furnished to, or created by or on behalf of, the Receiving Part y. Notwithstanding the return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Return of Confidential Information. Promptly following the request of the Disclosing Party, the Rece iving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party all copies and ex tracts thereof furnished to, or created by or on behalf of, the Receiving Part y. Notwithstanding the return of these materials, the Receiving Party will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the text segment 'all confidential information' creates ambiguity as the definition of such is not fully agreed upon, due to a previous perturbation. This contradicts section 3.1, which states that the Receiving Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Term and Termination. Notwithstanding whether or not the Proposed Transactions are concluded, the Receivi ng Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly refers to 'all confidential information', while the changed text omits 'all confidential information', only referencing 'all copies and extracts thereof'. This introduces ambiguity because the definition of 'confidential information' is now unclear, particularly if a previous perturbation affected the definition. This directly contradicts section 3.1, which obligates the Receiving Party to maintain the Confidential Information in confidence. The omission weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality obligation outlined in section 3.1, satisfying the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition, and creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Term and Termination. Notwithstanding whether or not the Proposed Transactions are concluded, the Receivi ng Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Term and Termination. Notwithstanding whether or not the Proposed Transactions are concluded, the Receivi ng Party shall maintain the information in accordance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'the Confidential Information in confidence' contradicts the entire purpose of the agreement, established in the opening paragraphs (WHEREAS clauses), which emphasize the importance of protecting Confidential Information. It weakens the enforceability of the entire agreement. The reference to confidentiality in WHEREAS clauses are no longer adressed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, the Bidder is interested in participating in the disposal process being undertaken by Transnet in relation to the business of Luxrail (hereinafter referred to as “the Blue Train” or “the Target”) (such disposal hereinafter refe rred to as the “Proposed Transaction”); AND WHEREAS, it is agreed that in order to enable the Bidder to assess the Target, Transnet will disclose Confidential Information relevant to the Proposed Transaction to the Bidder; AND WHEREAS, the Bidder recognises that careful protection and non-disclosure by the Bidder of Confidential Information disclosed by Transnet is of vital importance to the prosperity of the Target, Transnet and clientele of Transnet;
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the Receiving Party must maintain 'the Confidential Information in confidence'. The changed text removes 'the Confidential Information in confidence', weakening the obligation to protect the information. This directly contradicts the WHEREAS clauses, which emphasize the 'vital importance' of non-disclosure and careful protection of Confidential Information. Therefore, the modified text creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.7 Copyrights and Proprietary Rights. The Receiving Party shall not remove any copyright or proprietary rights notice atta ched to or included in the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 Copyrights and Proprietary Rights. The Receiving Party shall not remove any copyright or proprietary rights notice.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'atta ched to or included in the Confidential Information' creates ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of this clause, creating an in-text contradiction. The lack of a direct connection to Confidential Information, previously defined, now has no clear indicator of whats under what conditions the Copyrights and Proprietary Rights clause is applicable, and may conflict with previous section, 1.3, about the Waiving of rights to intellectual property
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.3 Ownership. The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in the Confidential Information and any reasonably related information which may be subsequently developed, and the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual pr operty and copyright therein in favour of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified that the copyright notice applied to information 'attached to or included in the Confidential Information.' By removing this qualifier, the changed text broadens the scope of the copyright clause, potentially applying it to any copyright or proprietary rights notice, even those unrelated to the Confidential Information. This contradicts section 1.3, which explicitly assigns all intellectual property rights in the Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party. The ambiguity created by the change leads to uncertainty about which copyrights are waived, creating a direct conflict and contradiction.
